coredata.dc:title: Optimization of high efficiency blue emissive N-, S-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Tiras, Kevser Sahin
Soheyli, Ehsan
Sharifirad, Zeynab
Mutlugun, Evren
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with bright emission at short wavelengths have attracted much attention due to their importance in various applications such as light-emitting diodes. During or after synthesis, several parameters can significantly improve the optical properties of GQDs. This study presents a facile solvothermal method with low-cost precursors using glutamic acid as the carbon source to realize blue emitting GQDs. The positive effects of urea and 1-octanethiol as nitrogen and sulfur dopants on the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the prepared GQDs were demonstrated and optimized. The results confirmed the formation of 2.2 nm nanoparticles with a bright emission around 381 nm with a full width at half maximum of 58 nm and a PLQY approaching 70 %. The decay lifetime of the emission also showed a tri-exponential profile with an average lifetime of 2.4 ns. The simplicity of the preparation method without any post-treatment process, together with a high PLQY of 70 % at short wavelengths, nominates the prepared GQDs for optoelectronics and UV light-driven biological purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solvothermal
Graphene quantum dots
Blue emission
High emission quantum yield

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of the prepared GQDs (sample prepared with 2 mmol glutamic acid, 1.1 mmol urea, and 1.2 mmol 1-octanethiol, after 8 h of solvothermal reaction at 200 °C). (B) EDX profiles of samples prepared at the presence of 0, 1.2, and 2.4 mmol of 1-octanethiol (samples prepared with 2 mmol glutamic acid, 1.1 mmol urea, and X mmol 1-octanethiol, after 8 h of solvothermal reaction at 200 °C). And (C) FTIR spectrum of the prepared GQDs (sample prepared with 2 mmol glutamic acid, 1.1 mmol urea, and 1.2 mmol 1-octanethiol, after 8 h of solvothermal reaction at 200 °C).

(A) TEM image of GQDs (size distribution columnar plot), (B) UV–Vis, PLE and PL spectra of the prepared GQDs (prepared with 2 mmol glutamic acid, 1.1 mmol urea, and 1.2 mmol 1-octanethiol, after 8 h of solvothermal reaction at 200 °C). Inset shows the Gaussian fitting of the PL spectrum under
λ
e x c
= 330 nm.

UV–Vis and PL spectra of GQDs prepared at different reaction times of 4, 8, 12, and 18 h. Insets show the photographs of as-prepared samples including 1 h (Sample prepared for 1 h was not soluble in any solvent).
UV–Vis and PL spectra of GQDs prepared at different amounts of 1-octanethiol.
(A) TCSPC profiles of GQDs prepared at different amounts of 1-octanethiol. (B) Exponential fitting results of a typical sample (1.2 mmol). Inset: lifetime components of each sample.

(A–C) UV–Vis and PL spectra of GQDs prepared at different amounts of urea (Inset shows the images of GQDs prepared with different amounts of urea, under daylight and 365 UV lamp). (D) Integrating sphere PLQY measurement for the optimized sample with 2 mmol glutamic acid, 1.2 mmol 1-octanethiol, 1.1 mmol urea, and 30 mL toluene, after reaction for 8 h at 200 °C.
Illustrative scheme of the synthesis process (Created via Biorender.com).
Details of the experimental parameters used during the optimization.
Optical emission characteristics of the prepared GQDs and comparing their results with recent reports.

head.ce:title: Optimization of high efficiency blue emissive N-, S-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with bright emission at short wavelengths have attracted much attention due to their importance in various applications such as light-emitting diodes. During or after synthesis, several parameters can significantly improve the optical properties of GQDs. This study presents a facile solvothermal method with low-cost precursors using glutamic acid as the carbon source to realize blue emitting GQDs. The positive effects of urea and 1-octanethiol as nitrogen and sulfur dopants on the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of the prepared GQDs were demonstrated and optimized. The results confirmed the formation of 2.2 nm nanoparticles with a bright emission around 381 nm with a full width at half maximum of 58 nm and a PLQY approaching 70 %. The decay lifetime of the emission also showed a tri-exponential profile with an average lifetime of 2.4 ns. The simplicity of the preparation method without any post-treatment process, together with a high PLQY of 70 % at short wavelengths, nominates the prepared GQDs for optoelectronics and UV light-driven biological purposes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent colloidal nanoparticles, which were recently recognized with the Nobel Prize in Chemistry, represent a diverse class of functional semiconductor materials characterized by a range of geometries and physicochemical properties resulting from quantum confinement effects. Among these, graphene-based quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as a novel category, distinguished from traditional colloidal quantum dots by their enhanced environmentally friendly attributes and unique photophysical properties, including broad emission spectra and significant Stokes shifts [1,2]. Furthermore, GQDs exhibit well-functionalized surfaces that allow for the precise tuning of optical properties across the entire visible spectrum [3,4].

Over the past decade, significant efforts have been directed toward developing low-cost preparation methods tailored to the available instrumentation and specific applications in fields such as medicine, optoelectronics, sensing, and photocatalysis [5–8]. Among these methods, high-pressure autoclave-assisted synthesis has emerged as a particularly effective technique for producing highly luminescent graphene-based quantum dots (GQDs). This method is advantageous as it does not require air-free systems and can facilitate the synthesis of GQDs in both aqueous and organic solvents. Given that the primary structural component of GQDs consists of carbon chains, a wide array of precursors—ranging from synthetic materials to natural sources such as plants—can be utilized to fabricate luminescent carbon-based quantum dots [9–12]. Besides, they are capable of accepting appropriate dopants like sulfur and nitrogen within their carbonic lattice or at the surface, leading to the enhanced PL emission properties [13].

Usually, carbon-based quantum dots with PL emission at short wavelengths of visible region represent a cyan emission at around 440–460 nm [14,15]. Luo et al., prepared tiny co-doped carbon dots with blue emission at 442 nm by introducing N–S- dopant ions, as bright luminescent probes for bioimaging and baicalein [16]. Researchers reported highly luminescent S, N co-doped GQDs synthesized via citric acid, urea, and thiourea with PLQY of 71–78 % at 435 nm and average decay lifetime of 12.8 ns, suitable for visible light photocatalysis [17]. Another study reported that organic fluorophores are the main source of luminescent characteristics in S,N-doped carbon dots with bright emission at 418 nm and emission lifetime of 9 ns (PLQY = 64 %) [18]. However, approaching the shorter wavelengths at deep-blue/UV regions has acquired a great interest which requires an improved synthesis approach. In another study, Hu's group suggested a hydrothermal method to prepare samples with violet PL signal at 382 nm [19]. However, they showed a low PLQY record of about 8.4 %. Therefore, representing a simple method with optimized photophysical properties, to realize high PLQY at wavelengths typically shorter than 410 nm is still challenging.

In the current study, GQDs were synthesized from glutamic acid and urea in toluene using a straightforward solvothermal method (see Scheme 1 ). The resulting GQDs exhibited strong photoluminescent (PL) emission at approximately 381 nm, achieved through the optimization of various synthesis parameters. A systematic investigation was conducted to evaluate the influence of experimental variables, including reaction time and dopant concentrations, to ensure the reproducibility of the GQDs. The simultaneous incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur dopants, utilizing urea and 1-octanethiol, respectively, led to enhanced PL emission at the same wavelength of 381 nm, with a remarkable photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) ranging from 65 % to 70 %, alongside a three-exponential decay profile of the excited states. The capacity of these GQDs to efficiently absorb ultraviolet light, combined with their high emission characteristics, positions them as promising candidates for applications in optoelectronics and biomedicine.

Materials. The glutamic acid (≥99 % Aldrich), 1-octhanethiol (98.5 % Aldrich), urea (BioXtra, Sigma-Aldrich), toluene (EMPLURA, Merck), and ethanol (EtOH, ≥99.5 %, Tekkim) were used as received for the synthesis of GQDs.

Synthesis method. In general, a mixture of glutamic acid (2 mmol), urea (0.6- 1.1–1.6 mmol), and 1-octanethiol (0- 0.6- 1.2–2.4 mmol) were added to a Teflon-lined autoclave including 30 mL of toluene, followed by heating at 200 °C for different time periods of 1, 4, 8, 12, and 18 h. The details of reactions have been summarized in Table 1 . After natural cooling down to room temperature, the black precipitations were gathered and washed with EtOH and water. Then, they were completely dissolved in EtOH using an ultrasonic bath. Finally, the 0.22 μm PTFE filter was used to filter the solution. A schematic of the synthesis process has been drawn in Scheme 1. All the optical measurements were performed after this step.

Characterizations. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was collected via Bruker D8 Discover. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed using the FT-IR spectroscopy model NICOLET 6700 made by a Thermo-scientific company. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was captured by Philips CM30 electron microscope (operating voltage of 300 kV). The EDX elemental analysis was performed by Thermo Fisher Scientific equipped via ELECT plus detector and Gemini 300 microscope. The optical spectroscopies were performed via UV–Vis (Thermo Genesys 10S spectrometer), PL/PL excitation (PLE) (Cary Eclipse), time-resolved PL (TRPL) (PicoQuant Fluo Time 200 time-correlated single photon counting system- TCSPC), and integrating sphere PLQY measurement (Quantaurus-QY instrument made by Hamamatsu Company).

Fig. 1 A shows a sharp and intense diffraction peak at around 26o which is attributed to the (002) planes of graphitic lattice (JCPDS No. 41–1487) in GQDs [20,21]. The sharp peaks imply the presence of graphitic and hexagonal carbons of (100)/(101) planes, representing of a high-degree of aromatic ring condensation [22]. After initial preparation of GQDs doped with nitrogen, long carbon-chain 1-octanethiol was added as a sulfur source to further improve the PL emission properties. In order to demonstrate that, the FESEM-based EDX profiles were captured and plotted in Fig. 1B at different amounts of 1-octanethiol. Along with the presence of C, N, and O as structural elements, results demonstrated that upon introducing 1-octanethiol, the sulfur-related weak signal emerges and its content increases with increase in the amount of 1-octanethiol. It means, 1-octanethiol with a relatively long carbonic chain and SH functional group works as a sulfur source. Indeed, at high temperature and pressure used during the solvothermal method, the thiol groups will gradually decompose, introducing small amounts of sulfur. GQDs are well-known for their rich surface functional group. The functional groups on the surface of the GQDs were analyzed through the FT-IR spectroscopy. As can be observed from Fig. 1C, GQDs display characteristic broad absorption bands of N–H and O–H stretching vibration at around 3196 and 3325 cm−1, respectively [23]. The tiny bands at 2840, 2944, and 3080 cm−1 are assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of sp3 (C–H), and vibrations of the aromatic ring of sp2 (C–H), respectively. The stretching vibration bands of CO, and C–CC are exhibited at 1654 and 1410 cm−1, respectively. The appearance of tiny bands related to the stretching vibration of S–H and S–C at 2525 cm−1 and 718 cm−1, respectively [24], indicated the incorporation of sulfur into GQDs structure/surface. The bands at around 1290 cm−1 and 1105 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the C–N and C–O, respectively [25,26].

The morphology of the prepared GQDs was tested via TEM analysis Fig. 2 A. It revealed the formation of tiny spherical particles with average size of 2.4 nm. As a main physical property of luminescent GQDs, their optical properties were evaluated using UV–Vis absorption, PL excitation (PLE), PL, TCSPC, and integrating sphere PLQY measurements. Fig. 2B shows the UV–Vis, PLE, and PL spectra of the GQDs. In the absorption spectrum, two peaks were appeared; one strong UV absorption related to the π→π∗ transitions of CC (sp2 domain in sp3 carbonic lattice) at 204 nm known as core state and another one centered at around 334 nm assigned to the n→π∗ transitions of nonbonding electrons of the CO surface functional group to π∗ orbital of the carbon core. Almost identical peaks were recorded in the PLE spectrum under an emission wavelength of 381 nm. A nice overlap detected between UV–Vis absorption and PLE spectra imply higher emission efficiency [27]. The PL emission measurements show an intense peak located at 380–390 nm when excited at the range of 300–360 nm. The highest intensity obtained at λexc = 330 nm, close to the PLE transition peak. By fitting this plot, three peaks were perfectly fitted as shown in the Inset of Fig. 2B, implying the main role of three different energy states in recombination of excited charges. Upon increase in the excitation wavelength from 370 to 390 nm, an obvious red-shift in the PL spectrum to 446 nm was recorded along with a significant decrease in the emission intensity. Besides, the full-width at half maximum (FWHM) value is around 58 nm which is narrower than the conventional FWHM of carbon-based quantum dots (typically>80 nm). Therefore, with an absorption onset at around 360 nm, they are able to efficiently absorb UV light and efficiently emit photons at visible regions. These GQDs are capable of being utilized in fabrication of optoelectronic devices [19] and light-assisted antibacterial sources [28].

The experimental parameters used during the GQDs' reaction are quite determinative, governing access to the higher PLQY. In this study, reaction time and amounts of dopants (urea as nitrogen source and 1-octanethiol as sulfur source) were optimized. Fig. 3 shows the effect of reaction time on the UV–Vis/PL and PLQY results of GQDs prepared using glutamic acid and urea. After 1 h of reaction, a white product remained at the bottom of the reactor with a transparent solution of toluene (Middle of Fig. 3). The white product was not even soluble in EtOH. However, GQDs prepared after 4-to-18 h showed an intense PL emission at 382 nm along with main transitions centered at 204–214 nm and 334 nm. Such a short absorption onset confirms a wide bandgap. As has been demonstrated at the insets, with the increase in reaction time, the color of as-prepared solution changed from yellow to orange, showing the enhanced number of GQDs prepared after a longer time period. Increase in the absorbance of the second peak also confirmed our assertion. Evgeny et al., demonstrated that blue-emissive centers are usually created after 1–2 h of reaction [29]. The PLQY calculations were performed via comparative method [30], considering 9,10-diphenylanthracene as reference dye and using EtOH and cyclohexane as GQD's and dye solvents (Dye efficiency of 95 % with PL maxima at 406 and 427 nm). The formula used is:
φ
G Q D
φ
d y e
=
I
G Q D
I
d y e
×
A
d y e
A
G Q D
×
(
r
G Q D
r
d y e
)
2
at which φ, I, A, and r stand for PLQY, integrated emission (area under PL profile when excited at specific excitation wavelength), absorbance (at the specific excitation wavelength), and refractive indices of solvents (used to dissolve GQDs and standard dye), respectively. It should be noted that the intersecting wavelength in absorption spectra of GQDs and reference dye was fixed at around 354–356 nm, resulting in Adye/AGQD = 1. This wavelength was selected as the excitation wavelength for our measurements. The estimated data were calculated and mentioned at the inset of PL plots, indicating the highest PLQY of 57 % for GQDs prepared after 8 h.

Almost in all synthesis recipes used for GQDs, the presence of at least one dopant specie such as phosphorous, boron, nitrogen, and sulfur [31,32] is an efficient strategy to realize the bright emission. For this purpose, 1-octanethiol molecules with S–H functional entity (as sulfur dopant agent) was added at different amounts. Fig. 4 demonstrates that upon addition of 1-octanethiol and increase in the amount of 1-octanethiol, the calculated PLQY enhanced from 57 % to 70 % without any change in the wavelength of absorption and emission spectra. As it was demonstrated in the EDX profiles, decomposition of 1-octanethiol introduces small amounts of sulfur which results in a tiny peak at 431 nm of the UV–Vis absorption spectra compared to the sulfur-free CDs (Insets of Fig. 4A and B).

A TCSPC system was used to record the decay profile of PL emission and calculate the average lifetime of de-excitation of electrons from an excited to the ground state. Fig. 5 A shows the decay profiles of different samples prepared with 0, 1.2, and 2.4 mmol of 1-octanethiol. The decay curves are almost identical, showing three exponential components with time-scales of 0.37, 2.17, and 5.80 ns, which are attributed to the three recombination pathways and therefore, the presence of multi-emissive center (Fig. 5B). They can be attributed to the core, edge, and surface states, respectively, as fitted in Fig. 2B. However, the average lifetime calculated for all GQDs prepared with 0, 1.2, 2.4 mmol of 1-octanethiol were 2.4 ns as summarized at the inset of Fig. 5B. These short lifetimes are comparable with other reports [19,33–35], confirming the domination of core states as the main recombination pathways.

The concentration of urea, as the main nitrogen precursor, was also modified (Fig. 6 A–C). With change in the amount of urea from 0.6, 1.1, 1.6 mmol, there are no variations in the shapes and positions of absorption and emission spectra, except an increase in the relative intensity of absorption peak at around 334 nm, which implies the determinative role of nitrogen to enhance the transition probability at longer wavelengths. However, the corresponding PLQYs were 63, 70, and 60 %, respectively. Therefore, the highest emission efficiency was obtained for GQDs prepared with 2 mmol glutamic acid, 1.2 mmol 1-octanethiol, 1.1 mmol urea, and 30 mL toluene, after reaction for 8 h at 200 °C. To further confirm our assertion, the integrating sphere method was used to directly calculate the absolute PLQY value for the optimized GQDs. Fig. 6D shows the pattern, with PL signal centered at around 381 nm and PLQY of 65.2 %. This value is a little lower than the comparative method (70 %), most probably due to the difference in the PL peak position of the prepared GQDs (381 nm) and standard dye (406 and 427 nm). Inset of Fig. 6B shows the images of the optimized sample in EtOH, captured under daylight and 370 nm UV lamp, which further support the high emission efficiency. Table 2 compares the results obtained in the presented study with other reports, summarizing the optical characteristics of the GQDs.

In summary, low-cost and high-quality doped-GQDs with efficient emission peak in the violet/deep blue region of the visible spectrum (∼381 nm) with a blue emission under 365 nm UV irradiation were prepared via solvothermal method. Using the beneficial aspects of sulfur and nitrogen dopants, and optimization of reaction times, high PLQY of around 70 % was realized which is a high record compared to the other short wavelength-emitting carbon-based quantum dots. The elemental analyses proved the presence of small traces of sulfur dopants upon addition of 1-octanethiol, resulting in an improved emission efficiency. The measurement of radiative recombination lifetime, confirmed a fitting result of three exponential functions with average life-time of 2.4 ns which imply the main role of core-states as emission centers. Since the fabricated samples were able to effectively absorb light in the UV region along with a subsequent bright emission of light at 381 nm, they are potentially suitable for fabrication of photodetectors, blue light emitting diodes, and functionality in light-assisted bactericidal agents.

Kevser Sahin Tiras: Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization. Ehsan Soheyli: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Zeynab Sharifirad: Formal analysis, Investigation. Evren Mutlugun: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge TÜBITAK, Project 20AG026.
